source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
The directions and magnitudes of three known forces are as follows: $\mathrm{F}_{1}{=}20\mathrm{N}$, directed at a 37-degree angle to the horizontal, upward to the right; $\mathrm{F}_{2}\mathrm{=}6\mathrm{N}$, vertically downward; $\mathrm{F}_{3}\mathrm{=}10\mathrm{N}$, horizontally to the left. It is known that $\sin37^{\circ}=0.6$ and $\cos37^{\circ}=0.8$.

1. Find the magnitude of the resultant force $F_{all}$ of the three forces.
2. Find the direction of the resultant force $F_{all}$ of three forces.
source_intake_record.raw_answer_text:
$6\sqrt{2}N$
The direction is at a 45^{\circ} angle to the positive x-axis, pointing to the upper right.

image_dependency: An image asset exists and registry_passed=true.
However, question_text explicitly states all three force magnitudes and directions, plus sin 37° and cos 37°, so the computation appears solvable from text alone.
multi_step: Solving requires resolving F1 into horizontal and vertical components using the provided trigonometric values.
Then the x- and y-components from all three forces must be summed before computing resultant magnitude.
A further step is needed to determine the resultant direction from the net components.
verifiability: The expected answer has clear numeric targets: one magnitude and one direction.
answer_text provides both outputs explicitly: a magnitude and an angle/quadrant direction.
The result can be checked by deterministic vector addition from the quantities given in question_text.

decision_reasons: Assets are materially complete: question text, answer, and primary image are all present, and registry_passed=true with no recorded asset issues.
The sample has strong quality signals from initial scoring, especially high multi_step_score (0.81) and verifiability_score (0.93).
Core semantic content appears recoverable even if the image is not strictly necessary, because the question text reportedly contains the needed force values and trigonometric information.
Risk flags are present, but they do not indicate loss of core semantics; they are limited to answer-type metadata mismatch and uncertainty about true image dependence.
